questions.question:
Let p, q, r and s be four primitive statements. Consider the following arguments:
P: [(~p \/ q) /\ (r -> s) /\ (p \/ r)] -> (~s -> q)
Q: [(~p /\ q) /\ [q -> (p -> r)]] -> ~r
R: [[(q /\ r) -> p] /\ (~q \/ p)] -> r
S: [p /\ (p -> r) /\ (q \/ ~r)] -> q
Which of the above arguments are valid?
A. P and Q only
B. P and R only
C. P and S only
D. P, Q, R and S
questions.to_find: Which of the four propositional arguments P, Q, R, S are valid (tautologies)

understand.plain: An argument 'premises -> conclusion' is valid when it is impossible for all the premises to be true while the conclusion is false; equivalently the whole implication is a tautology. The fastest way to test each one is to assume the conclusion is FALSE and the premises TRUE, then chase the forced truth values. If you hit a contradiction the argument is valid; if you find one consistent assignment (a counterexample) it is invalid.

keywords.explain: The conditional (conjunction of premises) -> conclusion is true under every truth assignment, i.e. it is a tautology. No row of the truth table makes premises true and conclusion false.
keywords.example: Modus ponens [(p -> q) /\ p] -> q is valid: there is no way to make p, p->q true and q false.

keywords.explain: A single assignment of T/F to the variables that makes all premises true but the conclusion false. Finding even one proves the argument invalid.
keywords.example: For R, p=F, q=F, r=F makes both premises true yet r (the conclusion) false.

keywords.explain: a -> b is false only when a is true and b is false; also a -> b == ~a \/ b. Using this lets you read off forced values quickly.
keywords.example: ~p \/ q is just p -> q, so a true p forces q to be true.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='560' height='250' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='34' class='lbl ink'>Validity test: assume conclusion FALSE</text><rect x='20' y='52' width='120' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='34' y='78' class='lbl ink'>P</text><text x='150' y='78' class='sub ok'>valid (contradiction)</text><rect x='20' y='100' width='120' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='34' y='126' class='lbl ink'>Q</text><text x='150' y='126' class='sub no'>invalid: p=F,q=T,r=T</text><rect x='20' y='148' width='120' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='34' y='174' class='lbl ink'>R</text><text x='150' y='174' class='sub no'>invalid: p=F,q=F,r=F</text><rect x='20' y='196' width='120' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='34' y='222' class='lbl ink'>S</text><text x='150' y='222' class='sub ok'>valid (contradiction)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A four-row chart marking P and S as valid (assuming a false conclusion forces a contradiction) and Q and R as invalid, each with the counterexample assignment that breaks it.

given.aim: Determine which of the four conditional arguments are tautologies, i.e. logically valid.

terms.meaning: Four independent primitive (atomic) propositions, each true or false.
terms.example: p could stand for 'it rains', q for 'roads are wet', etc.
terms.connects: They are the only variables; an argument is valid iff it holds for all 2^k assignments of them.

terms.meaning: The conjunction inside the outer brackets that must all hold for the inference to apply.
terms.example: In S the premises are p, p -> r, and q \/ ~r.
terms.connects: If the premises can be true while the conclusion is false, the argument fails.

terms.meaning: The formula on the right of the outermost ->; what the argument claims must follow.
terms.example: S claims q follows; R claims r follows.
terms.connects: Validity is checked by trying to make this false while keeping premises true.

terms.meaning: A formula true under every assignment; the whole 'premises -> conclusion' must be one for validity.
terms.connects: Valid argument and tautological conditional are the same thing here.

given.plan: Test each argument separately: assume its conclusion is false, propagate forced truth values through the premises, and check whether the premises can all stay true. A surviving assignment is a counterexample (invalid); a forced contradiction means valid.

steps.title: Test P
steps.formula_raw: valid iff no row has premises = T and conclusion = F
steps.apply: Conclusion ~s -> q is false only when ~s = T (s = F) and q = F. Premise ~p \/ q = p -> q with q = F forces p = F. Premise p \/ r with p = F forces r = T. Premise r -> s with r = T forces s = T, contradicting s = F.
steps.note: No assignment makes premises true and conclusion false, so P is VALID.

steps.title: Test Q
steps.apply: Try p = F, q = T, r = T. Premise ~p /\ q = T. Premise q -> (p -> r): p -> r is T (since p = F), so q -> T = T. All premises true, but conclusion ~r = F.
steps.note: A counterexample exists, so Q is INVALID.

steps.title: Test R
steps.apply: Conclusion r false: set r = F. Premise (q /\ r) -> p: q /\ F = F, so the implication is T. Premise ~q \/ p: take q = F (or p = T) to make it T. With p = F, q = F, r = F both premises are true while r = F.
steps.note: A counterexample exists, so R is INVALID.

steps.title: Test S
steps.formula_raw: valid iff no row has premises = T and conclusion = F
steps.apply: Assume conclusion q = F. Premise p = T. Premise p -> r with p = T forces r = T, so ~r = F. Premise q \/ ~r = F \/ F = F, which violates a premise.
steps.note: Premises cannot all be true when q is false, so S is VALID. Hence only P and S are valid.

solution.result: P and S are valid; Q and R are invalid. The correct option is C.

questions.formula_note: The key idea is the validity-as-tautology test: assume the conclusion false and the premises true, then propagate forced values; a forced contradiction proves validity (P, S) while any consistent assignment is a counterexample proving invalidity (Q, R).

formulas.name: Validity of a Propositional Argument (Tautology Test)
formulas.latex: (P_1 \wedge P_2 \wedge \cdots \wedge P_n) \rightarrow C \;\text{ is a tautology}
formulas.plain: An argument with premises P_1,...,P_n and conclusion C is valid iff the conditional (P_1 /\ ... /\ P_n) -> C is true under every truth assignment; equivalently, there is no assignment making all premises true and C false.
formulas.whenToUse: Use to decide whether a propositional argument or rule of inference is logically valid: assume C false and the premises true, then chase forced truth values. A contradiction proves validity; a consistent assignment is a counterexample proving invalidity.

terms.means: the i-th premise (a propositional formula that is assumed true)
logical implication; A -> B is false only when A is true and B is false
a formula true for every assignment of its variables

formulas.trap: Validity does not require the conclusion to be true on its own; it only requires that whenever all premises hold, the conclusion holds. Finding the premises satisfiable is not enough, and finding the conclusion sometimes true is irrelevant.
formulas.reference: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications - Rules of Inference and Validity of Arguments
